markdown:
# Ostrengarr

![Ostrengarr Harbor Festival](/lore/images/ostrengarr.png)

Those who know Ostrengarr only by old war songs know nothing worth trusting. The province has suffered more than most from being remembered by its former enemies, who found it easier to describe orcs as a storm than as a people. A traveler arriving with such notions soon discovers their foolishness. Ostrengarr is not a wasteland of raiders, nor a frontier held together by fear. It is a warm and fertile province of rainforest roads, tidal harbors, painted halls, inland rivers, white coasts, and communities so thoroughly rooted in shared custom that outsiders often mistake their confidence for leisure.

Yet Ostrengarr is not soft. It is simply civilized according to its own measures. Music carries law here. Public speech is an art and therefore a political weapon. A host who welcomes you may later defeat your argument in song before a hundred witnesses. A village feast may settle a dispute that would elsewhere require a judge, and a mural upon a longhouse wall may record a lineage or treaty more faithfully than many clerks' ledgers.

The wise explorer comes to Ostrengarr with open ears. Much of the province cannot be understood by looking alone. One must listen: to drums across a bay at dusk, to market recitations, to harbor chants, to funeral songs, to teasing verses traded between rival households, to children memorizing history that elsewhere would molder in archives. Ostrengarr is full of speech, but very little of it is careless.

Its ports ensure that Ostrengarr is never culturally alone, however. Landred grain ships arrive alongside Auromass charter vessels, Nex metalwork travels in holds beside lacquered timber and spice, and the colder stories of Fildren come south as sailors' warnings repeated over cups they swear are their last.

## Of the Character of the Province

Ostrengarr lies in the warmer middle of Ishredon, where the air grows heavy with salt, rain, flowering growth, wet timber, and spice. The coasts are bright and broad in places, broken by mangrove inlets, storm-lashed capes, fishing strands, and white beaches that make foreigners think briefly and incorrectly that life here must be easy. Inland, the country deepens into humid forest, red earth roads, river basins, fruit groves, hardwood stands, and scattered hill country where mist hangs low at dawn and drums carry strangely far.

The orcs of Ostrengarr live alongside others in port wards, craft towns, and trading enclaves, but their influence shapes the entire province. Clan memory remains important, though not in the crude sense imagined abroad. Lineage matters here because obligation matters. Kinship, patronage, shared teachers, sworn artistic houses, and public reputation all bind people together. A person may rise by talent, but they are expected to carry others with them when they do.

Magic, where it appears in Ostrengarr, is treated with respect but not with longing. The province trusts practiced skill more than miracle. Shipwrights, carvers, navigators, singers, herbalists, and engineers hold far more daily authority than any hedge-sorcerer. Old shrines persist, and some jungle places are said to be older than any living memory, but Ostrengarr in the present is held together by custom, labor, and the unembarrassed belief that beauty is part of order.

At the center of its public life stands the legacy of Wild Fritz Aled, the half-orc king whose rule is still spoken of not only in proclamations and civic records, but in remembered verses. Whether one calls him poet-king, reformer, or opportunist depends largely on where one was born, but none deny that he reshaped the province. Under his influence, Ostrengarr learned to prize performance not as ornament to power, but as one of its forms.

This makes Ostrengarr difficult for other provinces to read correctly. Landred often mistakes ceremony for ease, Nex mistakes lyricism for looseness, and Auromass sometimes mistakes beauty for inefficiency until its own negotiators discover how firm a smiling harbor council can be. Fildren alone is spared many such misunderstandings, largely because almost no one in Ostrengarr is fool enough to speak lightly of the north.

## The Great Places of Ostrengarr

### Aled's Rest

The chief city of Ostrengarr is Aled's Rest, a harbor capital built where deep water, sea trade, and public ceremony meet. It rises in terraces above a broad bay lined with piers, painted warehouses, boatyards, music courts, and council halls open to the sea breeze. The city is loud in a different manner than Deerness or Harrowgate. Here the noise is not merely commercial but deliberate: harbor calls, sung decrees, drum relays, rehearsal yards, and public debates all ring through the streets.

Travelers expecting a grim martial capital are usually disarmed by the city's color. Cloth awnings, painted hulls, carved lintels, tiled courtyards, and salt-bright walls give Aled's Rest a theatrical quality, though its business is perfectly real. Spice, hardwood, fruit, salt fish, dyes, shells, sailcloth, and worked ivory from lawful trade all pass through its docks. So too do envoys, wandering performers, opportunistic merchants, and more smugglers than the harbor watch cares to admit.

### Drumbay

South along the coast lies Drumbay, perhaps the most famous port in the province after the capital. It is a seafarers' town of ropewalks, net lofts, smokehouses, dry docks, and sea taverns where half the room is laughing and the other half is negotiating freight. The bay earns its name from the old drum towers that once relayed warning inland during pirate seasons and now still sound for arrivals, storms, and festivals.

Drumbay is a place where one may hire a captain, lose a purse, hear six contradictory stories about the same shipwreck, and eat better fish than in any city of the north.

### Rainspire

Rainspire stands inland where river trade meets the forest edge. Built upon high ground with long stilted storehouses and covered walkways, it is a town of timber counts, fruit brokers, resin merchants, caravan factors, and scribes who pretend they are not moved by poetry even while quoting it. Heavy weather shapes the place. Roofs are steep, drains are deep, and every traveler learns within an hour why locals dress for rain before the sky has made up its mind.

### Whitehook

Whitehook clings to a bright cape of chalk and stone above treacherous surf. Pilots, signal keepers, and weather-readers make their living there, and every tavern wall is marked with the names of ships that did not round the hook twice. Whitehook is admired for its beauty and respected for its funerals.

## Towns of River, Coast, and Canopy

### Saltwake

A hard-working salt town of pans, drying sheds, shallow boats, and labor browned by sun and brine. It is not picturesque up close, though painters love it from a distance.

### Brightwater

Known for freshwater springs and orchard country near the coast, Brightwater sends fruit preserves, sweet wines, and cooling cordials across the province. Visitors often stay longer than planned, which locals claim is the water and outsiders blame on the hospitality.

### Kora Fen

A marsh-edge trading town where reed craft, fish markets, herbal trade, and suspiciously discreet river traffic all prosper together. Many strangers dislike Kora Fen on first meeting and miss it later.

### Embervine

Embervine lies amid rich red soils and heat-loving vineyards. It is known for strong drink, loud festivals, and a civic habit of turning even minor disagreements into memorable public performances.

### Tallroot

Built among great trees and raised walkways, Tallroot trades hardwood, lacquer, carved goods, and medicinal bark. It is one of the places foreigners most often imagine when they speak vaguely of Ostrengarr, though the real town is less mystical and much busier.

### Seastrand

A long white-beach town where fishing fleets, shell gatherers, sail makers, and pearl speculators crowd the same strand. Beautiful from the dunes, noisy in the market, and ruinously expensive during festival season.

### Flint Quay

A smaller cargo port with a sharp commercial temper. Less charming than Drumbay and more useful to those who prefer straightforward freight, warehousing, and guarded transactions.

### Veya Crossing

A river-crossing town inland from the coast, known for ferries, drovers' yards, and the sort of bridge arguments that can occupy three generations if nobody sensible intervenes.

## Villages and Lesser Stops

### Greenveil

A forest village of growers, gatherers, and basket makers tucked beneath broad leaves and slow rain. Quiet from afar, deeply observant up close.

### Sunnet

A fishing village on warm shallows, known for woven nets, coral charms, and a beach shrine where sailors leave offerings before long voyages.

### Red Palm

A fruit-growing village of red earth, drying racks, and family compounds with painted walls. Its produce is excellent and its news travels alarmingly fast.

### Hollow Reed

Set near marsh and inlet, Hollow Reed lives by trapping, reed work, and ferrying. It also produces more songs per household than seems mathematically possible.

### Broad Canoe

A river settlement where boatbuilders, polemen, and traders meet before journeys inland. Every child there can tie a knot before they can write a letter.

### Lantern Grove

A village built around an old gathering place hung with lights during feast days. Foreigners remember its beauty. Locals remember who embarrassed themselves there last year.

### Three Drums

A crossroads settlement named for the drum posts that once marked distance and warning. It now serves as an inn cluster, exchange point, and favorite meeting ground for itinerant performers.

### Clifffruit

A difficult little settlement clinging to terraced slopes above the sea, known for hardy citrus, stubborn goats, and people who refuse to move somewhere easier out of pure principle.

## Halls, Courts, and Sacred Places

### The Hall of Open Verse

In Aled's Rest stands the Hall of Open Verse, where public recitations, civic disputes, memorial performances, and formal declarations are made. To outsiders it may resemble a theater. In practice it is often closer to a court.

### Fritz Aled's Court

Whether palace, residence, or ceremonial seat, the court of Wild Fritz Aled remains one of the symbolic hearts of the province. It is a place of carved timber, shaded courts, disciplined attendants, and the dangerous possibility that one might say something foolish within hearing of people eloquent enough to preserve it forever.

### The Sea Courts

These open-sided civic halls near the harbor hear trade cases, docking disputes, salvage claims, and maritime grievances. Proceedings can be dry, until suddenly they are not.

### House Merrow

A respected shipbuilding and navigation house whose yards and training schools have shaped coastal trade for decades. Their apprentices are overworked, their masters vain, and their vessels excellent.

### The Painted Cloister

A religious complex deep in humid garden country, known for painted walls, scriptoria, disciplined hospitality, and a tradition of preserving history through image as well as text.

### Shrine of the First Tide

An old coastal shrine where offerings are made for safe departure and rightful return. Mariners who laugh at it publicly often visit it privately.

## Wild Country, Old Places, and Rumored Troubles

### The Singing Canopy

A great stretch of rainforest where wind, insects, birds, and distant river sound produce such shifting noise that travelers swear the trees are speaking. Guides say this is nonsense, though they often lower their own voices while passing through.

### The White Beaches

Beautiful, dangerous, and often misleading. Some are easy strands near friendly villages. Others conceal rip currents, reef teeth, smugglers' caches, or burial places older than the province's current name.

### Stormglass Reef

A shattered coastal reef feared by pilots and prized by wreck divers, pearl seekers, and every fool who thinks the sea owes them one good fortune.

### The Old Drum Towers

A chain of weathered signal towers along coast and high ground, once used for warning and coordination. Some still function. Others have become shrines, lookout posts, lovers' meeting places, or dens for those who prefer old stone to honest rent.

### Rain Mother's Steps

A stepped waterfall and shrine complex inland, revered by some as sacred and by others as merely beautiful. Both groups tend to arrive at the same time, which causes difficulty.

### The Broken Palisade

Remnants of an old war line swallowed by root and vine. Children play there, elders avoid it, and treasure seekers insist there must be something buried beneath the earthworks.

### Black Current Inlet

A narrow tidal inlet much favored by smugglers, secret meetings, and stories that begin with someone insisting they knew a shortcut by water.

## Counsel for the Traveler

Come to Ostrengarr ready to listen more than you speak, and never assume that silence means consent or that laughter means mockery. You will eat well, hear better music than you deserve, and learn that hospitality can coexist very comfortably with scrutiny. Praise a craft honestly, pay ferrymen fairly, show respect in shrines even when you do not share the belief, and do not attempt to outdrink harbor musicians unless you wish to become a local anecdote.

Do not come expecting the province to confirm northern prejudices. Ostrengarr is too old, too capable, and too self-aware for that. Its strength does not always resemble rank upon rank of armored men, though it can muster those when required. More often it appears in the resilience of its roads, the memory of its songs, the discipline of its shipyards, the confidence of its councils, and the settled knowledge that a people need not imitate foreign notions of refinement in order to possess one of the richest cultures in Ishredon.

*Edward Pennywhistle - Explorer Extraordinaire*
